figures.caption: Fig. 5: Qualitative comparison of unknown object prediction. Images are from Lost&Found [45]. Annotated unknown objects are outlined in orange and are reflected in the quantitative evaluation, while additional valid but unannotated ones are outlined in yellow. Predicted unknown objects are shown in red. Compared to P2F [51], which often merges OOD stuff regions or misses unknown instances, our method produces more consistent instance-level predictions of unknown objects.

Fig. 6: Open-set Panoptic Segmetation Results. Images are from MS COCO [32]. Annotated unknown objects are outlined in orange and are reflected in the quantitative evaluation, while additional valid but unannotated ones are outlined in yellow. Predicted unknown objects are shown in red. Qualitative results demonstrating Hyp2Former’s ability to detect unknown objects while segmenting known classes.

Fig. 3: Real-world zero-shot evaluation. Unknown objects are marked in red. Without fine-tuning, Hyp2Former generalizes to unseen real-world scenes and reliably detects unknown objects under distributional shift.
